⚠ Hourly fetch is slower (~10s for 5Y)
Click Run to start. First call fetches BTC history (~2s).
📋 Methodology (click to expand)

Two modes:

· Calendar cycle: opens a new trade every N days, holds until profit target / stop loss / DTE-0 settlement. Uses daily candles. Best for testing the "set it and forget it" cadence (e.g. bi-weekly strangle).

· Time window: opens a trade every week at a specific day-of-week + time-of-day IST (e.g. every Friday 23:30 IST), holds for a fixed number of hours, then closes regardless of expiry. Uses hourly candles. Best for testing event-driven workflows like the Friday-night strangle.

Data: BTC OHLC from Binance (BTCUSDT spot), live-fetched and cached for 1 hour. Calendar mode uses daily candles (~1 fetch call); time-window mode uses hourly candles (~44 fetch calls for 5Y).

IV proxy: Each trade's IV = trailing 30-day realized volatility × multiplier. Real BTC IV historically runs ~5–15% above realized (volatility risk premium). Default 1.10 captures most of this; raise to 1.20+ for more realistic short-vol pricing, lower to 1.00 for a conservative stress test.

Strike selection: Same logic as planner — Black-Scholes delta-matching using BS pricing at the trade's IV.

Mark-to-market: Legs are repriced at each candle close using BS with the new spot, shrinking T, and held-constant IV (no IV path simulated). This means a sudden IV expansion — common around real-world shocks — is NOT modeled. Real losses on those days were larger than this backtest shows.

Time-window granularity: The hourly bucket covering "23:30 IST" is the 23:00–24:00 IST candle. Entry mark uses that candle's close (≈24:00 IST). For 23:30-precision intraday entries, this introduces up to ~30 min of timing slack — material on event days, negligible most other days.

Exit rules: Hit profit target (% of credit), hit margin-based stop loss (% of margin), or — calendar mode: settle to intrinsic at DTE 0; time-window mode: close at hold-duration end at the prevailing mark.

Fees: 0.05% taker × 4 legs (or 2 for naked) per round trip, on notional. STT/CTT not modeled (Delta India fee structure varies).

What this backtest IS good for: Comparing strategies, parameter sensitivity (delta choice, target %, stop %), regime detection (which years/months were brutal vs friendly), validating event-driven setups (Friday-night strangle, post-FOMC entries).

What it is NOT: A precise P&L predictor. Real option chains have skew, term structure, and IV jumps that this synthetic model cannot replicate.

IV Z-Score (Augen)
—
−2σ (IV Crash)−1σ0σ (Avg)+1σ+2σ (IV Rich)
📖 How to use this for expiry selection & IV decisions ▾
1️⃣ Find your edge — read these 3 columns first
IV% = what the market is paying. HV30% = what realised over 30 days. RV5% = last 5-day realised. The IV−HV column is your edge: +15%+ = RICH (sell), +5–15% FAIR, <+5% POOR.
2️⃣ Pick the expiry — sweet spot is 7–21 DTE
0d = pure gamma roulette, theta is huge but one move kills you. 1–4d = best theta-to-vega ratio if IV is rich. 7–21d = institutional sweet spot, smooth decay, room to manage. 30d+ = vega-heavy, IV move dominates P/L.
3️⃣ Confirm with IVR — only sell when IV is statistically high
IVR > 60 = sell with conviction. 40–60 = wait or half-size. <40 = IV is at the low end of its 52-week range; selling here means you're short cheap vol — skip.
4️⃣ 24h POP / ROM — only after you execute a trade
Once you execute a strangle in the Short Strangle Simulation Desk below, this column shows three margin-based metrics for that position: POP-24h (probability of profit), E[ROM] (expected % return on the margin you posted), and ⚠ MC% (probability of margin-call). All on Delta India PM @ 200× basis. POP ≥70% + E[ROM] >+10% is a keeper; MC ≥15% is a yellow flag.
⚠ Decision rule: The EDGE BOX at the bottom is your master gate — if it says POOR, DO NOT sell premium even if a single row looks rich. Sinclair's framework requires all conditions to align: IV-HV gap, IVR, VoV, and IV percentile.
Premium quality per expiry: RICH = IV−HV >15% ✅ Sell · FAIR = 5–15% ⚠ Wait · POOR = <5% ❌ Skip
ExpiryDTEIV%HV30%RV5%IV−HVIV−RVIVRStraddle $Theta/DayQualityAction24h POP / ROM

Euan Sinclair's Volatility Trading rules for short straddle entry. All 8 must pass for full conviction.
Loading…
Fetching data
Jeff Augen's method: Express IV changes in standard deviations using 20-day sliding window. The Z-Score tells whether current IV is statistically elevated (sell) or depressed (wait).

⏳ Building multi-timeframe history… results sharpen as more samples accumulate.
What this shows: VoV measures how much ATM IV itself wobbles — the "speed of vol changes." Low VoV = IV is stable, premium decay is smooth, short strangles work cleanly. Rising VoV often precedes IV regime breaks: premiums whip around, your .10 delta legs can drift to .20+ even without big spot moves. A falling VoV during a high-IV regime is the real green light for selling.

⏳ Recording theta history for the selected expiry — multi-timeframe trend appears as samples accumulate.
How to read: Theta should grow more negative as DTE bleeds (faster decay near expiry). ▲ RISING theta (less negative) = decay is slowing — usually means IV repriced higher or you're crossing into a flatter section of the decay curve. ▼ FALLING = decay is accelerating — gamma's catching up. Reaction/min = how much theta moved per minute on average across the window — a useful sensitivity check before adjusting strikes.
All Expiries — IV · HV · RV · Straddle · Greek Danger Status (ATM Short Straddle Seller View)
Note: For ATM option seller — 14 DTE = Optimal. 7→0 DTE = Danger Zone (Gamma accelerates). Expiry resets daily at 5:30 PM IST.
IV = Implied Vol (options market) HV = 30-Day Historical Vol RV = 5-Day Realized Vol (Parkinson) Edge % = IV − HV · green ≥ +10% · amber ≥ +5% · red < +5%

No active strangle position detected.
Once you fire a short strangle from the simulator (or auto-trade fires),
this panel will compute Probability of Touch, Breakeven Cushion, VRP edge
and the final go/no-go score.
📞 Short Call—
Delta—
PoT (≈2Δ)—
Distance—
BE cushion—
📉 Short Put—
Delta—
PoT (≈2Δ)—
Distance—
BE cushion—
🎯 Any-Touch
—
prob a leg is tested
σ Expected Move
—
1σ to expiry
📏 Cushion / σ
—
tighter leg in σ
💎 VRP Edge
—
IV − RV vol pts
How to read: PoT ≈ 2 × |Δ| (industry rule of thumb for one-touch through expiry). BE cushion = distance from spot to breakeven, divided by 1σ expected move (σ_$ = spot × IV × √(DTE/365)). A healthy strangle has cushion ≥ 1.0σ on both legs, any-touch ≤ 50%, and VRP > 5 vol pts. Score combines all four.

⏳ Building dealer-positioning history… cells fill as samples accumulate.
How to read: When spot is above zero-gamma, dealers are net-long gamma → they sell rallies, buy dips → range-bound = short strangles favoured. Below zero-gamma, dealers chase moves → trends extend = strangle killer. Max Pain is the strike where most options expire worthless; price often gravitates toward it into expiry. Strangles with shorts inside the call/put walls have higher win rates.

Loading funding & basis…
Fetching from Binance / Delta perp APIs
💸 Funding (Binance)
—
per 8h · annualized
📊 Avg Funding 24h
—
3-period mean
🌀 Basis (perp-spot)
—
% premium
📈 OI Δ24h
—
perp open interest
⚡ Squeeze Score
—
0–100 (higher = risk)
🎚 Position Bias
—
long/short crowding
How to read: Sustained funding above +0.05%/8h (annualized ≈55%+) means longs are paying heavily — crowded long, vulnerable to long-squeeze (puts get tested hard). Sustained negative funding = crowded short, short-squeeze risk (calls breached). Best short-strangle window: funding near 0 (±0.01%) and basis under 5% annualized — neither side is paying premium = balanced book = sideways range likely. Watch for funding flip — sign-change preceded by OI drop = forced deleveraging in progress.

Interpolating IVs at 25Δ from chain
🪞 25Δ Risk Reversal
—
IV(25P) − IV(25C)
🦋 25Δ Butterfly
—
avg wing − ATM
📍 ATM IV
—
at-the-money
📉 25Δ Put IV
—
downside skew
📈 25Δ Call IV
—
upside skew
🎯 Wing Edge
—
paid for tails
📋 Skew Snapshot
MetricValueReading
Awaiting chain data…
How to read (BTC convention): 25Δ Risk Reversal = IV(25Δ Put) − IV(25Δ Call). In BTC, put-skew is normal (3–8% positive RR). Steep RR (>10%): fear bid → puts rich → put-side credit attractive but means market expects downside, size down. Flat/inverted RR (≤0): euphoria — call-skew unusual = squeeze risk = short call leg dangerous. 25Δ Butterfly measures how much wings cost above ATM — high BF means tails are expensive (good for sellers), low/zero BF means market complacent (selling tails undervalued = caution).

📊 Kurtosis (30D)
—
3 = normal · >5 = fat tails

—
largest 1D move (σ-units)
↔ Skewness (30D)
—

🎯 Strangle Penalty
—
size adjustment
📅 Last 30 days — daily moves (σ-units)
Loading…
📋 Per-Expiry Guidance — Jump Risk
Loading expiries…
Why this matters for short strangle: BTC's biggest losses for premium sellers come from jumps, not drift. A normal-distribution model under-prices tail risk when realized kurtosis is high. Reading: kurtosis <4 + 0–1 jumps = clean tape, full size OK · kurtosis 4–6 OR 2–3 jumps = jumpy, halve size · kurtosis >6 OR ≥4 jumps = unstable, skip naked strangles, use iron condors. The 30D vs 90D ratio shows whether jump risk is rising into your trade.

🔍 ±5 strikes around candidates — spread distribution
Loading…
📋 Per-Expiry Guidance — Liquidity
Loading expiries…
Why this matters: Even a perfect macro setup loses edge if you eat 5–8% of premium on entry slippage. Reading: spread <2% = institutional liquidity, full size OK · 2–4% = retail-grade, fine for small size · 4–7% = thin, expect slippage · >7% = avoid this strike, walk one strike closer to ATM where quotes tighten. Combined OI <500 contracts = high adjustment cost on rolls.

📈 25Δ RR — last 7 days (auto-tracked)
📋 Per-Expiry Guidance — Skew Momentum
Loading expiries…
Why this matters: Skew level alone is misleading — what matters is direction. RR steepening +2pp in 24h with flat spot = institutions buying downside hedges before something happens. Reading: RR steepening >+2pp/24h = halve size on the put side or skip · steepening on flat tape = strongest signal · RR flattening = complacency, normal sell · RR inverting (negative) = call-side squeeze risk, dangerous to short calls. History persists in localStorage.
⏳
Computing forward variance…
Comparing your expiry vs the next one for relative-value
🎯 Front IV (T1)
—
your expiry
📅 Far IV (T2)
—
next expiry
🔭 Forward IV
—
implied T1→T2 vol
⚖ Front vs Fwd
—
premium/discount
📐 Slope
—
vol pts / day
🎯 Verdict
—
expiry choice
📋 Per-Expiry Guidance — Forward Vol
Loading expiries…
Forward variance = √[(σ²_T2 × T2 − σ²_T1 × T1) / (T2 − T1)] — the implied vol the market is pricing for the period between your expiry and the next one. If front IV >> forward IV: your expiry is trading rich, sell it (favorable). If front IV ≈ forward: calendar is flat, no edge in choosing one over the other. If front IV << forward: next expiry has the richer premium, consider rolling out instead. Event-driven IV usually concentrates in one expiry — this catches that.
